---
title: "Using because-py directly in Python"
description: |
  How to call the because Python API directly for integration into
  Python-native workflows.
execute:
  eval: false
---

## Overview

because-py exposes a `fit()` function that accepts formula strings and NumPy arrays.
This is useful when you want to integrate causal modelling into a Python pipeline
(e.g., alongside pandas, scikit-learn, or ArviZ).

## Basic usage

```python
import because
import numpy as np

# Simulate some data
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
n   = 200
x   = rng.normal(size=n)
y   = 0.7 * x + rng.normal(size=n)

result = because.fit(
    equations   = ["y ~ x"],
    data        = {"x": x, "y": y},
    num_samples = 1000,
    num_warmup  = 500,
    num_chains  = 4
)

# Posterior summary
import arviz as az
idata = az.from_numpyro(result["mcmc"])
print(az.summary(idata, var_names=["beta_y_x", "alpha_y"]))
```

## Specifying families

Pass a `family` dictionary to use non-Gaussian likelihoods:

```python
result = because.fit(
    equations   = ["y ~ x + z"],
    data        = {"x": x, "z": z, "y": y_counts},
    family      = {"y": "poisson"},
    num_samples = 2000
)
```

Supported families: `"gaussian"`, `"poisson"`, `"binomial"`, `"negbinomial"`, `"ordinal"`.

## D-separation testing

```python
dsep_result = because.fit(
    equations = ["Brain ~ Mass + Migration",
                 "Lifespan ~ Brain",
                 "Clutch  ~ Brain + Mass + Migration"],
    data      = data_dict,
    dsep      = True,
    dsep_only = True   # skip MCMC, only run d-sep tests
)

for test in dsep_result["dsep"]:
    print(test)
```

## Accessing the generated model code

```python
result = because.fit(
    equations   = ["y ~ x"],
    data        = {"x": x, "y": y},
    num_samples = 1000
)

# Print the auto-generated NumPyro model
print(result["model_code"])
```

## Using ArviZ for diagnostics

```python
import arviz as az

idata = az.from_numpyro(result["mcmc"])

# Trace plots
az.plot_trace(idata)

# R-hat and ESS
print(az.summary(idata))

# WAIC
print(az.waic(idata))
```

## GPU / multi-device sampling

JAX handles device placement automatically. To run 4 chains on 4 GPUs:

```python
import jax
print(jax.devices())   # check available devices

result = because.fit(
    equations  = ["y ~ x"],
    data       = data_dict,
    num_chains = 4,
    n_cores    = 4
)
```
